title: Variety’s Education Impact Report Spotlights Innovation and Storytelling as Schools and Students Adapt to Changing Biz Landscape
description:
AFILos Angeles

	AFI boasts a student-to-faculty ratio of 6:1, with instructors comprised of active industry professionals such as James Mangold and
body:
AFILos Angeles

	AFI boasts a student-to-faculty ratio of 6:1, with instructors comprised of active industry professionals such as James Mangold and Len Amato. The school embraces new technology and innovation in its curriculum, including what they call “the Sandbox — a collaborative lab where technology partners and AFI fellows work side by side in real production settings,” says Susan Ruskin, dean, AFI Conservatory/exec vp. “The Amazon Innovative Storytellers Initiative is the Sandbox in action: a program that embeds second-year fellows in a hands-on exploration of how AI can serve — and expand — the art of independent filmmaking in their own thesis films.” Alum Autumn Durald Arkapaw recently became the first woman to win an Oscar for cinematography.




	

					Belmont University
	



	Belmont UniversityNashville, Tenn.
	

	Belmont’s Nashville location is ideal. “This creative, fast-moving city functions as a living classroom, and it’s often hard to tell where Belmont ends and the industry begins. That’s intentional,” says Brittany Schaffer, dean of the Mike Curb College for Entertainment & Music Business. “Through classes like Bonnaroo U, initiatives like Dolly U (our partnership with Dolly Parton and her team), and co-curricular experiences like the Showcase Series, our students work directly with artists, creators, agencies, vendors, and entertainment companies while they’re still in school. They see firsthand how projects are developed and brought to life.” Recognizing the need for balance, the school launched the Center for Mental Health in Entertainment. One of the most popular classes on campus is Social & Emotional Wellness in the Entertainment Industry. “We’re committed to shaping a healthier, more sustainable entertainment industry where creative excellence and mental well-being coexist, and our students are eager to help build that future with us.”

	Biola University, Snyder School of Cinema & Media ArtsLa MiradaThe school prepares students for the entertainment industry of today and tomorrow by bringing in professionals from the business — it also emphasizes “qualities such as adaptability, reliability, collaboration,” says dean Tom Halleen. “If we can prioritize excellence in those areas, too, along with the craft, they will be better prepared for this ever-changing industry, because they’ll know how to adapt.” While the school, located just down the freeway from L.A., enjoys top facilities and a robust curriculum, it’s getting ready to open its 45,000-sq.-ft. Snyder studio center, “intended to house the full production workflow, from ideation to pre-production, production to post-production all the way through presentation within the state-of-the-art theater,” says Halleen. And they need the space, he says — enrollment is up 63% since just prior to the COVID lockdown.
	

	Brooklyn College, the Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema

	Brooklyn, N.Y.

	Feirstein takes advantage of the booming New York production scene; it’s located on the Steiner Studios lot, giving students access to four soundstages, a mixing facility exclusive to Feirstein, a recording studio and ADR suites. The school offers two degree programs: the Master of Arts in cinema studies and the Master of Fine Arts in cinema arts. Students typically make at least four films before they graduate, while a thesis film class provides students with mentor support and funds. “We recognize that AI is here, and that it will significantly impact areas like advertising and visual effects. At the same time, we believe the core of cinema remains unchanged. Audiences come to film for a shared human experience — the feeling of sitting together in a theater, and the emotional connection that comes from recognizing ourselves in the characters and in the artists who create them. For that reason, we see AI not as a replacement for storytelling, but as a tool that can expand what storytellers are able to do. The ability to tell meaningful, human stories will remain essential, something technology can enhance, but not replicate,” says associate professor and executive director Charles Haine.

	Cal State Northridge

	Northridge

	An affordable gem in the north San Fernando Valley, CSUN offers students practical, hands on production and media management courses and experience. “We focus on producing ‘complete filmmakers’ — creatives who are technically proficient but also understand the business and the evolving nature of global distribution,” says Jared Rappaport, chair, Dept. of Cinema and Television Arts. “We are moving toward a model that emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration, ensuring our students aren’t just masters of a single craft, but are ‘hyphenate’ creators — writer-director-producers — who can navigate the shifts from virtual production to independent transmedia storytelling.”
	

	Chapman University, Dodge College of Film & MediaOrange

	Boasting the 76,000-sq.-ft. Marion Knott Studios, the Digital Media Arts Center and Chapman Studios West, a 38,000-sq.-ft. facility, Chapman gives students the tools to dive right in with production, from screenwriting to post, animation and VFX. Lightning-quick adaptability keeps the school current with showbiz trends. “This university is much more flexible … it’s always had a very entrepreneurial spirit,” says dean Stephen Galloway, noting that they have a streamlined process for adding classes to the curriculum. “We introduced, for instance, six new classes related to AI. We brought in experts in each area to train the faculty — such as Rob Legato, who’s won three Oscars for visual effects — on how to use this so that they could train the students. I think we’ve been very much the cutting edge in these new areas.”



	

					Cherokee Film Institute
	



	Cherokee Film Institute

	Owasso, Okla.

	The first of its kind, the institute offers hands-on training in all aspects of production so graduates can seamlessly transition onto soundstages and studios. “Authentic Native American representation has been close to nonexistent since Hollywood’s inception,” says Brettlyn Bevenue, program manager, Cherokee Film Institute. “Our mission at CFI is to train, develop and elevate Native American and local talent to increase representation in film and media. We’ve seen a positive shift within the past decade and that conversation is how we begin our curriculum.” She notes that students get practical experience in the burgeoning Oklahoma production scene: “Our classes are held at the Cherokee Film Studios campus, so not only are students working with industry-standard technology, but they’re exposed to both practical production and virtual production on our soundstages.”

	Columbia College Chicago
	

	Chicago, Ill.

	Columbia, which boasts a 35,000-sq.-ft. soundstage and Volume wall among its facilities, recently added a screenwriting and development MA, which is currently accepting students for the fall. Columbia now also offers a Film and Television BA and welcomes DGA member Johnson Cheng (“American Born Chinese”) to its faculty of entertainment industry professionals. Its Semester in L.A. program includes weekly, guest-driven lectures with industry prods on the Fox lot. Featured speakers include veteran writer Andrew Orenstein (“Malcolm in the Middle”), Leonard Dick (“Murder in a Small Town”), and producers Todd Berger and Carol Baum. Adding to its luster: alum Chase Infiniti, who took awards season by storm with her breakout performance in “One Battle After Another.”

	Columbia University School of the Arts

	New York City

	With MFA degrees offered in film, theater, visual arts and writing, and an M.A. in film and media studies, Columbia is a stand-bearer in arts education. The school prides itself on rigorous grounding in storytelling that “future proofs” its students, according to a statement. The school’s film faculty comprises working professionals, who bring real-world solutions into the classroom. Its suite of digital storytelling courses and integration with the School of the Arts Digital Storytelling Lab keep Columbia ahead of the curve of changes in technology. Other graduate film programs are just beginning to experiment with AI, but Columbia has been teaching about it since before the pandemic, according to a statement.

	DePaul University

	Chicago

	Film and TV production, animation, VFX — DePaul’s curriculum is built around “experiential learning, with students and faculty working side by side on projects that mirror professional workflows,” says Daniel Klein, co-chair of the Master of Fine Arts in Film and Television Directing and director of Industry Engagement. The students get hands-on experience at the Cinespace Chicago soundstage, working alongside projects such as “The Bear” and Dick Wolf productions. DePaul also hosts an annual AI in Film Education conference, bringing together filmmakers, technologists and educators “to explore how these tools are being used in professional workflows and how that should inform film education,” says Klein. The goal is to integrate these new tools but emphasize that “a deep understanding of film language, storytelling and core texts of the discipline is what allows students to use these tools in a meaningful and professional way.”

	Emerson College
	

	Boston

	The former Visual and Media Arts department is now the School of Film, Television, and Media Arts, opening up more career paths for students. The move includes the hiring of a campus dean at Emerson Los Angeles, Dr. Ken Rogers, establishing a strong bicoastal approach. Students have access to industry-standard soundstages, labs and production suites, while addressing new AI technology. “Students appreciate that Emerson incorporates traditional filmmaking. We are one of the only higher education institutions in the country to teach filmmaking from the industry’s roots in analog 16mm film technology to present-day integrations, such as virtual reality and AI,” says Shaun Clarke, interim dean, School of Film, Television and Media Arts. “At its core, the truly holistic approach to all facets of the business distinguishes Emerson in the marketplace, and students feel that in everything from the course offerings to the guest lectures and the campus experience.”

	Hofstra University

	Hempstead, N.Y.

	The Lawrence Herbert School of Communication at Hofstra’s motto of “hands-on from day one” describes its undergraduate program, which combines small classes with real-world experiential learning in nearby New York City, and a semester in Los Angeles. As tech advances, so does the curriculum — a new course, Gen AI for Film and Television, allows students to use popular apps to experiment with storytelling, pre-visualization, production, and post-production workflows. Students also develop a critical and ethical understanding of how AI is impacting the creative industry. Of course, the school’s deep bench of facilities and faculty give the next generation of storytellers a solid foundation. “Students want education that prepares them for a changing industry and one that actually believes and invests in us and our future careers,” says Diego Olivares (’26), BFA in filmmaking.

	Ithaca College, Roy H. Park School of Communications

	Ithaca, N.Y.

	The school offers students experience from day one in its three production studios, as well as a Volume stage, virtual production studios and broadcast television studios with industry grade equipment. “Because we’re a complete communications school and not just a film school, students can take complementary courses in advertising, public relations, sports media, documentary, advanced screenwriting for film and TV, arts/entertainment journalism, and emerging media,” says Amy Falkner, dean of the Park School of Communications. “Students want to be challenged. Specifically, they want access to young alumni working in the industry. They really appreciate seeing recent grads succeed. A perfect example is Dylan Clark ’23, whose short film ‘Portrait of God’ — written and directed during Clark’s time at IC — is now being developed into a feature film at Universal with Jordan Peele and Sam Raimi producing and Clark directing his feature script.”

	Loyola Marymount University School of Film and Television
	

	Los Angeles

	LMU’s SFTV expanded its partnership with Sony Electronics in 2025 to launch the Social Impact Filmmaking Lab. The initiative is designed to support recent alumni creating socially driven films by providing funding, mentorship and professional resources, including the latest Sony technology. While the Distinguished Artist in Residence and Presidential Fellow Programs continues to give students hands-on mentorship, the school has beefed up its hardware, having purchased more than $1.2 million of new equipment, including five Sony Venice 2 cameras and post-production equipment. The school has also added classes on AI and media production, including a collaboration course with its school of law. It counts Oscar-winning DP Autumn Durald Arkapaw as an alum — the art history undergrad took a genre film class that changed her career trajectory.

	New York Film Academy

	New York, Burbank

	Founded as an alternate to traditional four-year universities, the NYFA uses a “learn by doing” philosophy, with students in degree and certificate programs creating films from day one. It’s a very curated and practical array of studies, including filmmaking, acting, documentary filmmaking, screenwriting, editing, broadcast journalism, 3D animation and visual effects, game design, musical theater, cinematography and producing. With campuses on both coasts and Florence, Italy, as well as locations at Harvard, Kazakhstan, Paris and Beijing, it also offers international study. Students work with top-of-the-line equipment in professional facilities, with showbiz veteran faculty and career services.

	New York University

	New York City

	With a legendary list of alumni, NYU continues to attract, nurture and graduate filmmakers ready to take on the entertainment business. Students can use the state-of-the-art facilities at 370 Jay Street, the school’s Brooklyn center for media, art and technology, which houses the interactive telecommunication program, its interactive media arts program, the NYU Game Center and the Clive Davis Institute. The Martin Scorsese Institute of Global Cinematic Arts sports the Martin Scorsese Virtual Production Center and the Martin Scorsese Department of Cinema Studies. NYU alumni include Spike Lee, Chloé Zhao, Ang Lee, Dee Rees and Cary Fukunaga.

	Rochester Institute of Technology
	

	Rochester, N.Y.

	RIT president Bill Sanders notes the school “was one of the first universities in the country to offer virtual production technology and courses for our students” and the strong alumni network and the RIT in L.A. program helps students stay closely connected to the industry. “Our School of Film and Animation faculty are working professionals who are current with emerging needs who bring those real-world insights and connections directly into the classroom.” Given RIT’s history of being in the forefront of technology, “We believe it’s critical that generative AI does not lead us toward a future that exploits artists,” he says, noting that there’s continuous communication about AI with students and faculty. “We’re committed to making sure our students’ creativity remains first and foremost human-driven. That balance between technological innovation and human creativity has always been central to RIT, and that is not going to change.”

	Rutgers University, Mason Gross School of the Arts

	New Brunswick, N.J.

	Situated near the production hotbeds of New York City and northern New Jersey, Rutgers offers students a visiting filmmaker series, documentary lab and top-notch facilities. It’s also addressing the rapidly changing world of entertainment and tech. “Rutgers Filmmaking has developed an AI and New Technologies in Filmmaking course that introduces students to the evolving role of generative AI and emerging technologies in cinema,” says Patrick Stettner, chair, Mason Gross School of the Arts. “Students are intrigued by these new tools but are frankly wary about losing a sense of authorship. They are not rejecting the technology, but they have clear awareness about what feels authored and what feels machine-made and are trying to find a way to use it that still feels personal and appropriate. They want to protect what they value in filmmaking while they work out how these tools belong in their practice.”

	Savannah College of Art and Design

	Savannah, Ga.

	SCAD emphasizes collaboration and real-world production experience, so its curriculum reflects the ever-changing models of the entertainment industry, says Andra Reeve-Raab, dean, school of film and acting. “Our belief has always been we learn by doing,” she says, which includes an 11-acre backlot and LED Volume stages. SCAD students are creating content across various formats, including a horror anthology with the collaboration of VFX and animation students. “We’re our own production house,” she notes. “We’re not waiting for our students to get hired. We’re creating our own content, and that content just won 11 student Emmys — that’s a big deal.” The students and school are also embracing new forms of entertainment, like verticals, and “harnessing” AI for certain disciplines. Reeve-Raab adds that the school has a 99% job placement rate.

	Syracuse University
	

	Syracuse, N.Y.

	Syracuse University’s Newhouse School of Public Communications offers BS and MA degrees in television, radio and film, while the College of Visual and Performing Arts grants BFA and MFA degrees — both give students opportunities to create and learn everything about their chosen media, from screenwriting to post. “Film students everywhere are asking the same thing right now: give me the craft and give me the context to use it in the real world,” says Alex Méndez Giner, associate professor and program coordinator of film, VPA. He notes that the university is listening and adapting to real-world demands, including AI. “What we aim to build is discernment. Students must understand AI, interrogate it and deploy it responsibly— all in order to enhance their work but not replace the art and craft. Our job is to graduate storytellers, not prompt engineers,” says Tula Goenka, professor and director of the master’s of television, radio and film program, Newhouse.



	

					University of Arizona alum and Emmy Award winner Craig T. Nelson returned to collaborate with Film & Television students on the set of Be Still, Boone, the senior thesis film of student writer/director Clinton Willis, right/ Photo: Julius Schlosburg
									Julius Schlosburg
	



	University of Arizona, School of Film, Theatre and Television

	Tucson, Ariz.

	A Sitcom Lab allowed students to realize a sitcom and pitch it to industry pros, while last year, the school partnered with Film Independent on Film & Television Los Angeles, an outpost that gives student access and opportunities in the middle of the entertainment industry. “Students consistently tell us they value hands-on production, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and opportunities to work on projects that mirror professional practice,” says Dr. Frances Gateward, director of the School of Film, Theatre and Television. “Our filmmakers, actors, designers, writers and scholars collaborate on productions and performances throughout the year, building portfolios that demonstrate both creative vision and practical skill.” Students also take annual trips to the Sundance Film Festival and learn strategy for submissions. “Their films regularly screen at festivals in the U.S. and internationally, giving emerging filmmakers experience navigating the festival circuit, widening their industry networks, and expanding the audiences for their work.”
	

	UCLA, School of Theater, Film and Television

	Los Angeles

	Collaboration, career readiness and active learning balanced with wellness are some of the attributes of the school, which boasts award-winning alumni. TFT dean Celine Parreñas Shimizu emphasizes the active nature of the students’ education. “They learn from each other, and they become powerful thinkers together,” she says, while recognizing the need to adapt around tech like AI. “AI is a tool. Human creativity is the author.” She taps into a network of alumni and industry to bolster the curriculum, while diversity — in the student body and points of view — remains key. “I also know that we can transform the industry, right? And you can really see it here, you know, the inclusive excellence that is UCLA,” she says.

	University of North Carolina School of the Arts, UNCSA School of Filmmaking

	Winston-Salem, N.C.

	With the latest in production facilities — including emerging technologies — UNCSA stresses interdisciplinary collaboration in filmmaking, music, performance and visual arts. UNCSA continues to prioritize equity in filmmaking, supporting all student productions with funding and equipment. The schools of drama and filmmaking have partnered on a new One-Act Festival, producing a series of one-act plays that also become short films, while a nonfiction film expansion is led in part by director Molly Bernstein (“Art Spiegelman: Disaster Is My Muse”). Dean Deborah LaVine says, “One of the strengths of our school is that we embrace the idea of hybridity” in artists. She is also excited about Story Art Studio, which acts as an incubator for cross-disciplinary work in VFX, animation, puppetry, stop motion and immersive technology: “What kind of language does this build if you’re playing with all these different tools?”

	USC, School of Cinematic Arts



	

					USC School of Cinematic Arts/ Photo: Habib Zargarpour
									Habib Zargarpour
	



	Los Angeles

	With loads of famous alumni, top facilities and programs, USC attracts ambitious students from around the world. While storytelling remains a key component, students are “also aware that storytelling is a constantly evolving artform, so they are looking toward the future and new tools for storytelling, including virtual production, game/film hybrids and immersive experiences,” says Holly Willis, chair of the Media Arts + Practice division. “They are also interested in understanding industry trends, exploring new formats like verticals and podcasts, and connecting with audiences.” And changing technology is also top of mind. “In our research labs, we are also focusing on AI,” Willis says. “AI for Media & Storytelling (AIMS) has supported a long list of student projects exploring diverse genAI platforms, while the Ganek Immersive Studio has been exploring ways to integrate AI into VFX. And the Entertainment Technology Center is studying how to integrate genAI into professional filmmaking workflows with several projects recently completed or in progress.”
	

	U. of Texas at Austin, Moody College

	Austin, Texas

	Smack dab in the middle of a growing Texas production boom, Moody College has a long-standing relationship with the Texas Film Commission as well as various local studios and other biz shingles, opening doors for students looking for internships and other positions. The school just launched a digital game industries minor and the Matt Karch Gaming Institute in order to train students to be leaders in game production and development, says Cynthia McCreery, chair, Dept. of Radio-Television-Film. The school has also launched a sports production and broadcasting minor. “Students are trained in everything from studio to field production to editing and storytelling. The program is working with UT Athletics in giving students hands-on real-world experiences,” she says, adding that students worked on UT basketball broadcasts, producing the post-game shows.

	Vancouver Film SchoolVancouver, CanadaErika Herndier, head of education at Vancouver Film School, says that the school “continues to evolve its approach to provide flexible learning options (i.e., our new Hybrid Production Model), production-focused training, and a supportive, community-driven environment, ensuring students are not only prepared for the industry, but able to thrive within it.” VFS offers industry-standard facilities, while “we continuously integrate emerging tools and practices — from real-time engines and virtual production pipelines to evolving animation, sound and game design technologies — ensuring students are working with the same systems used in today’s studios.” And industry feedback is important. “We regularly consult with studio partners, alumni and hiring managers to identify skill gaps and anticipate future needs, allowing us to refine course content and introduce new offerings where necessary.”

title: Hulk Hogan Took Lethal Amounts of Fentanyl Daily After Divorce from Linda
description:
Hulk Hogan
                          Took Deadly Amounts of Fentanyl to Manage His Pain
body:
Hulk Hogan
                          Took Deadly Amounts of Fentanyl to Manage His Pain
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              April 22, 2026
                            3:57 AM PDT
                                                                      |
                                                                Updated 
                                  April 22, 2026
                                7:47 AM PDT
                                    
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Hulk Hogan was taking life-threatening amounts of fentanyl to manage his physical pain during his time with the TNA following his divorce from his wife, Linda Hogan.
      
    
          The wrestling pioneer detailed his reliance on the drug in the new Netflix docuseries "Hulk Hogan: Real American," which served as his final interview before his sudden death in July 2025 from a heart attack.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Hulk said he signed up for the TNA (Total Nonstop Wrestling, the second-largest wrestling group behind WWE) after he gave "everything" to Linda during their 2009 divorce... and needed the cash. But, it wasn't long before he and TNA realized he was in no shape to compete at the level he'd been contracted to, so he turned to the opioid to help his severe pain.
      

          Hulk explained ... “I was taking 80-milligram fentanyls, two in the morning, stuffing them under my gums here ... I had two 300mg patches of fentanyl on my legs and they gave me six 1500mg fentanyl lollipops to eat."
      
    
          He continued ... "I went to the pharmacy, he goes, 'You should be dead. We have never seen a human being take this much fentanyl.'"
      
    
          The wrestling legend got to the point of feeling so much pain, he had to sleep in a chair. He added ... "If I just twitched my finger like that, my whole back would spasm and torque."
      
    
          His last in-ring appearance was in 2012, and he departed the TNA in 2013 when his contracted expired.

title: The Delta 72 Frontman Gregg Foreman Dead at 53
description:
Singer Gregg Foreman
                          Dead at 53 ...
                          Founded A
body:
Singer Gregg Foreman
                          Dead at 53 ...
                          Founded Alternative Band The Delta 72
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              April 22, 2026
                            9:44 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Gregg Foreman -- a founding member of the alternative group The Delta 72 -- has died.
      
    
          Friends and collaborators of Foreman's -- including Wesley Eisold from the group Cold Cave -- confirmed the news ... remembering him as a truly unique star and passing along their condolences.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          A cause of death for Foreman has not yet been released.
      

          Foreman rose to prominence after forming The Delta 72 -- an alternative-rock band -- way back in 1994. The group's goal was to make an eclectic discography ... full of punk music, country and everything in between.  
      
    
          The group played together from 1994 to 2001 ... before the members split up to pursue other projects. Foreman joined the band Cat Power in 2006.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          He worked with many popular stars over the years ... including Jello Biafra of the Dead Kennedys, Kat Von D and even Michael Jackson's daughter, Paris.
      
    
          Foreman just finished touring with Cat Power in March ... and he wrote on Instagram, his new band was mixing and recording its first EP. It's unclear how far along in that process the group got prior to Foreman's death.
      
    
          He was 53.
      
    
          RIP

title: 'Family Matters' Star Bryton James' Ex-Wife Files for Spousal Support
description:
'Family Matters' Star Bryton James
                          Officially Divorced
body:
'Family Matters' Star Bryton James
                          Officially Divorced
                          ... Ex Wants Monthly Checks
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              April 22, 2026
                            7:54 AM PDT
                                                                      |
                                                                Updated 
                                  April 22, 2026
                                8:49 AM PDT
                                    
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Bryton James’ divorce may be officially over ... but the financial drama from it remains ongoing -- because his ex-wife is now asking the court to make him cut her checks for spousal support.
      
    
          According to new legal docs obtained by TMZ, the "Family Matters" star's ex, Jahaira James, filed a request for spousal support less than a month after their divorce was finalized.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          The former couple's divorce judgment was entered on March 24, and their marital status was officially terminated on Sunday -- restoring both parties to single status. The final judgment also show the court's jurisdiction to order spousal support to either party was terminated as part of the split.
      
    
          But now Jahaira apparently wants that revisited.
      

          In her new filing, Jahaira says she works as a professional dancer and performer whose income is anything but predictable. She claims her work revolves around live performances, rehearsals, touring gigs, and short-term contracts ... and says she doesn't have a salaried position or guaranteed ongoing income.
      
    
          She says the entertainment hustle comes with major instability ... explaining work depends on auditions, availability, and short-term bookings, causing her income to fluctuate from month to month.
      
    
          Jahaira also says staying competitive in her field requires constant auditions, training, and physical conditioning ... plus unpaid prep time and out-of-pocket costs like rehearsal space and audition-related expenses.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Her bottom line is she says she’s currently unable to earn enough to maintain the standard of living established during the marriage without temporary spousal support.
      
    
          Jahaira claims she earns about $1,150 per week before taxes. She also estimates Bryton pulls in more than $34K in gross income per month.
      
    
          A judge will now decide whether Bryton’s post-divorce peace gets interrupted by a new monthly bill.

title: David Wilcock, Paranormal Writer and YouTuber, Dead at 53
description:
Paranormal Writer David Wilcock
                          Dead at 53
body:
Paranormal Writer David Wilcock
                          Dead at 53
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              April 22, 2026
                            9:18 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
          David Wilcock, the American paranormal writer and YouTuber, has died ... TMZ has learned.
      
    
          Authorities in Boulder County, Colorado say deputies were dispatched Monday after a 911 call came in around 10:44 AM for an unknown problem just outside the town of Nederland.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
          Authorities say the emergency communications specialist who took the 911 call believed the caller might have been in the middle of a mental health crisis.
      
    
          Deputies arrived on scene and encountered a man outside a residence who was holding a weapon. Within minutes of their arrival, officials say the man used the weapon on himself. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
          Investigators say deputies searched the home and surrounding property and found no one else.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
          Detectives are now working alongside the Boulder County Coroner's Office to determine the official cause and manner of death.
      
    
          Wilcock was 53.
